description: Spec Authoring Validation: strip ensures clauses from exec functions, ask model to reconstruct them

details.gt_ensures:
ensures
    (r as nat) == (x as nat) * (y as nat),
    r <= u128::MAX,
details.generated_ensures:
ensures
    r == x as u128 * y as u128,
    r <= u64::MAX as u128 * u64::MAX as u128,
details.analysis: Core spec (r = x*y) is semantically equivalent: nat multiplication vs u128 multiplication both correct since product fits in u128. Second clause: GT has trivially-true r <= u128::MAX; generated has tighter but also derivable bound u64::MAX^2. Both are essentially redundant information clauses. Semantically equivalent.

details.gt_ensures:
ensures
    output == spec_add_fe51_limbs(self, _rhs),
    fe51_as_nat(&output) == fe51_as_nat(self) + fe51_as_nat(_rhs),
    fe51_as_canonical_nat(&output) == field_add(fe51_as_canonical_nat(self), fe51_as_canonical_nat(_rhs)),
    fe51_limbs_bounded(self, 51) && fe51_limbs_bounded(_rhs, 51) ==> fe51_limbs_bounded(&output, 52),
    fe51_limbs_bounded(self, 52) && fe51_limbs_bounded(_rhs, 52) ==> fe51_limbs_bounded(&output, 53),
details.generated_ensures:
ensures
    output == spec_add_fe51_limbs(self, _rhs),
    fe51_as_nat(&output) == fe51_as_nat(self) + fe51_as_nat(_rhs),
    fe51_as_canonical_nat(&output) == field_add(fe51_as_canonical_nat(self), fe51_as_canonical_nat(_rhs)),
details.analysis: First 3 clauses match GT exactly. Missing the 2 bound-propagation clauses (51-bit inputs => 52-bit output, 52-bit inputs => 53-bit output). These are critical for callers that chain field operations and need to track limb growth. Model correctly inferred the structural/algebraic spec from add_assign but missed the bound-propagation pattern.

details.gt_ensures:
ensures
    fe51_as_nat(&r) == u8_32_as_nat(bytes) % pow2(255),
    fe51_limbs_bounded(&r, 51),
    is_uniform_bytes(bytes) ==> is_uniform_field_element(&r),
details.analysis: from_bytes_post(bytes, &r) is defined as exactly fe51_as_nat(&r) == u8_32_as_nat(bytes) % pow2(255), so clause 1 is semantically equivalent. Clauses 2-3 are exact matches. Model found the bundled predicate instead of spelling out the property directly — valid alternative abstraction level.

details.gt_ensures:
ensures
    fe51_limbs_bounded(self, 52),
    u64_5_as_nat(self.limbs) == 16 * p() - u64_5_as_nat(old(self).limbs) - p() * ((36028797018963952u64 - old(self).limbs[4]) as u64 >> 51),
    field_canonical(u64_5_as_nat(self.limbs) + u64_5_as_nat(old(self).limbs)) == 0,
    self.limbs == spec_negate(old(self).limbs),
details.generated_ensures:
ensures
    self.limbs == spec_negate(old(self).limbs),
    fe51_limbs_bounded(self, 52),
    (u64_5_as_nat(self.limbs) + u64_5_as_nat(old(self).limbs)) % p() == 0,
details.analysis: 3 of 4 GT clauses captured: spec_negate structural equality, 52-bit bound, and algebraic negation identity ((a+b) % p == 0, using % p() which is equivalent to field_canonical(...) == 0). Missing: the detailed intermediate formula showing the exact nat value as 16p - old_nat - p*(carry). This formula is important for downstream proofs that need to reason about the exact representation, not just the field-canonical value.

details.gt_ensures:
ensures
    r.limbs == spec_reduce(limbs),
    fe51_limbs_bounded(&r, 52),
    (forall|i: int| 0 <= i < 5 ==> limbs[i] < (1u64 << 51)) ==> (r.limbs =~= limbs),
    fe51_as_nat(&r) == u64_5_as_nat(limbs) - p() * (limbs[4] >> 51),
    fe51_as_canonical_nat(&r) == u64_5_as_field_canonical(limbs),
    fe51_as_nat(&r) < 2 * p(),
details.generated_ensures:
ensures
    r.limbs == spec_reduce(limbs),
    fe51_limbs_bounded(&r, 52),
    fe51_as_canonical_nat(&r) == u64_5_as_field_canonical(limbs),
    fe51_as_nat(&r) < 2 * p(),
details.analysis: 4 of 6 GT clauses captured correctly. Missing: (1) identity-when-already-reduced (forall|i| limbs[i] < 2^51 ==> r.limbs =~= limbs) — important for callers that know their input is already reduced; (2) exact nat relationship fe51_as_nat(&r) == u64_5_as_nat(limbs) - p() * (limbs[4] >> 51) — needed for detailed carry analysis downstream.

details.gt_ensures:
ensures
    is_valid_edwards_y_coordinate(field_element_from_bytes(&self.0)) <==> result.is_some(),
    result.is_some() ==> (
        edwards_y_nat(result.unwrap()) == field_element_from_bytes(&self.0)
        && edwards_z_nat(result.unwrap()) == 1
        && is_well_formed_edwards_point(result.unwrap())
        && (field_square(field_element_from_bytes(&self.0)) != 1 ==> edwards_x_sign_bit(result.unwrap()) == (self.0[31] >> 7))
    ),
details.generated_ensures:
ensures
    result.is_none() <==> !is_valid_edwards_y_coordinate(field_element_from_bytes(&self.0)),
    result.is_some() ==> is_well_formed_edwards_point(result.unwrap()),
    result.is_some() ==> edwards_y_nat(result.unwrap()) == field_element_from_bytes(&self.0),
    result.is_some() ==> edwards_z_nat(result.unwrap()) == 1,
    result.is_some() ==> (edwards_x_sign_bit(result.unwrap()) as u8) == (self.0[31] >> 7),
details.analysis: All GT properties are covered (validity iff Some, well-formed, Y matches, Z=1, X sign bit). However, the X sign bit clause is unconditional while GT correctly conditions it on field_square(y) != 1. When y^2 == 1, x = 0 and negation is the identity, so sign bit is always 0 regardless of the compressed sign bit. The generated spec would reject valid implementations or fail to verify. Also has a spurious 'as u8' cast on edwards_x_sign_bit. Classification: too_strong (overly restrictive sign bit guarantee).
Exact match. Model correctly identified spec_ristretto_compress as the reference spec function and the .0 field accessor for CompressedRistretto.

details.gt_ensures:
ensures
    group_canonical(product_of_scalars(old(inputs)@)) != 0 ==> is_inverse_of_nat(&result, product_of_scalars(old(inputs)@)),
    group_canonical(product_of_scalars(old(inputs)@)) != 0 ==> forall|i: int| 0 <= i < inputs.len() ==> #[trigger] is_inverse(&(#[trigger] old(inputs)[i]), &(#[trigger] inputs[i])),
details.generated_ensures:
ensures
    inputs@.len() == old(inputs)@.len(),
    product_of_scalars(old(inputs)@) % group_order() != 0 ==> (
        forall|j: int| 0 <= j < inputs@.len() ==> #[trigger] is_inverse(&old(inputs)@[j], &inputs@[j])
    ),
    product_of_scalars(old(inputs)@) % group_order() != 0 ==> is_inverse_of_nat(&result, product_of_scalars(old(inputs)@)),
details.analysis: Core properties correct: uses % group_order() which is semantically equivalent to group_canonical(...). Both inverse properties (element-wise and product) are captured. Extra clause: inputs@.len() == old(inputs)@.len() — this is always true for &mut slice operations but GT doesn't include it. Minor trigger differences (old(inputs)@[j] vs old(inputs)[i]). Classification: too_strong (adds unnecessary length invariant).

details.gt_ensures:
ensures
    is_valid_montgomery_point(result),
    montgomery_point_as_nat(result) == montgomery_scalar_mul_u(
        spec_x25519_basepoint_u(),
        scalar_as_nat(&Scalar { bytes: spec_clamp_integer(bytes) }),
    ),
details.generated_ensures:
ensures
    is_valid_montgomery_point(result),
    montgomery_point_as_nat(result) == montgomery_scalar_mul_u(
        spec_x25519_basepoint_u(),
        scalar_as_nat(&Scalar { bytes: spec_clamp_integer(bytes) }),
    ),
details.analysis: Exact match. Model correctly identified is_valid_montgomery_point, montgomery_scalar_mul_u, spec_x25519_basepoint_u, scalar_as_nat, and spec_clamp_integer. Also correctly used scalar_as_nat (not spec_scalar) since clamped values can exceed group_order.

details.gt_ensures:
ensures
    !choice_is_true(choice) ==> result.limbs == a.limbs,
    choice_is_true(choice) ==> result.limbs == b.limbs,
details.generated_ensures:
ensures
    !choice_is_true(choice) ==> result.limbs == a.limbs,
    choice_is_true(choice) ==> result.limbs == b.limbs,
    !choice_is_true(choice) ==> fe51_as_canonical_nat(&result) == fe51_as_canonical_nat(a),
    choice_is_true(choice) ==> fe51_as_canonical_nat(&result) == fe51_as_canonical_nat(b),
    (fe51_limbs_bounded(a, 54) && fe51_limbs_bounded(b, 54)) ==> fe51_limbs_bounded(&result, 54),
details.analysis: First 2 clauses match GT exactly. Additional 3 clauses are logically derivable from limbs equality but not in the GT: fe51_as_canonical_nat preservation (trivially follows from limbs equality) and bound propagation (also trivially follows). These won't break anything but add unnecessary proof obligations. Classification: too_strong (adds redundant derived properties).

key_findings.can_infer_ensures: Yes, model correctly infers ensures from function bodies in 7/10 cases (capturing all GT properties or more). Fails to capture all properties in 3/10 cases (missing bound-propagation and detailed intermediate formulas).
key_findings.over_specification_patterns: Model tends to add derivable properties (S10 adds canonical_nat from limbs equality), add unnecessary invariants (S8 adds length preservation), and remove important conditions (S6 removes sign-bit guard). The S6 case is the most concerning — unconditional sign bit could cause verification failure.
key_findings.under_specification_patterns: Model consistently misses bound-propagation clauses (S2 missing 51->52 and 52->53 bit propagation) and detailed intermediate formulas (S4 missing carry formula, S5 missing identity-when-reduced and exact nat relationship). These are 'implementation-aware' specs that callers need for chaining operations.
key_findings.spec_function_usage: Model correctly identifies and uses spec functions in all 10 cases. Uses equivalent alternatives in some cases (from_bytes_post vs direct expression, % p() vs field_canonical) but these are semantically correct.
key_findings.complexity_correlation: Strong correlation: simple (1/1 correct), medium (3/6 correct), complex (0/3 correct). Complex functions with branching (decompress), mutation (negate), or batch operations (batch_invert) are hardest.
